name: Nonmyeloablative HSCT delayed MS progression but remains a high-risk boundary
summary: A randomized trial found markedly less multiple sclerosis progression after nonmyeloablative HSCT than disease-modifying therapy, while preserving the disease-specific and intensive-procedure boundary.
statement: In relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, nonmyeloablative HSCT reduced disease progression events versus continued disease-modifying therapy, but the intervention is an intensive immune-ablation and reconstitution procedure for a disease population.
population_or_model: People with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis and recent relapses despite disease-modifying therapy
time_horizon: Median follow-up 2 years, with some annual follow-up through 5 years
quantitative_note: Disease progression occurred in 3 HSCT participants versus 34 disease-modifying-therapy participants; the hazard ratio for progression was 0.07 (95% CI 0.02 to 0.24; p < 0.001).
caveats: The population was younger relapsing-remitting MS patients, not older adults with stem-cell exhaustion.
HSCT requires conditioning and immune reconstitution, making it unsuitable as a preventive aging intervention.
The trial was preliminary and requires long-term safety and replication context for broader interpretation.
